content:
# Genesis Chapter 6 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 4r–4v | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_006_page_001.jpg, GEN_006_page_002.jpg
**Additional Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — LXX | **View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0008

---

**1** And it came to pass, when the human beings began to multiply on the face of the ground and daughters were born to them,

**2** that the sons of God saw that the daughters of the human beings were good, and they took for themselves wives from all whom they chose.

**3** And the LORD said, "My spirit shall not contend with the human beings forever, because he is flesh. And his days shall be one hundred and twenty years."

**4** The Nephilim were on the earth in those days, and also afterward, when the sons of God came in to the daughters of the human beings and they bore children to them. These were the mighty men who were of old, the men of renown.

**5** And the LORD saw that the wickedness of the human beings was great in the earth, and that every inclination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil all the day.

**6** And the LORD was grieved that He had made the human beings on the earth, and He was pained in His heart.

**7** And the LORD said, "I will blot out the human beings whom I have created from the face of the ground — from human being to beast, to crawling thing, to birds of the heavens — for I am grieved that I have made them."

**8** But Noah found favor in the eyes of the LORD.

**9** These are the generations of Noah. Noah was a righteous man; he was blameless in his generations. Noah walked with God.

**10** And Noah fathered three sons: Shem, Ham, and Japheth.

**11** And the earth was corrupt before God, and the earth was filled with violence.

**12** And God saw the earth, and behold, it was corrupt, for all flesh had corrupted their way upon the earth.

**13** And God said to Noah, "The end of all flesh has come before me, for the earth is filled with violence through them. And behold, I am about to destroy them with the earth.

**14** Make yourself an ark of gopher wood. Make rooms in the ark, and cover it inside and outside with pitch.

**15** And this is how you shall make it: the length of the ark three hundred cubits, its breadth fifty cubits, and its height thirty cubits.

**16** A tsohar you shall make for the ark, and to a cubit you shall finish it from above, and the door of the ark you shall set in its side. Lower, second, and third decks you shall make it.

**17** And I — behold, I am bringing the flood of waters upon the earth to destroy all flesh in which is the breath of life from under the heavens. Everything that is on the earth shall die.

**18** And I will establish my covenant with you, and you shall come into the ark — you, and your sons, and your wife, and your sons' wives with you.

**19** And from every living thing, from all flesh, two of every kind you shall bring into the ark to keep them alive with you. They shall be male and female.

**20** From the birds according to their kinds, and from the beasts according to their kinds, from every creeping thing of the ground according to its kind — two of every kind shall come to you to keep them alive.

**21** And you, take for yourself from every food that is eaten and gather it to yourself, and it shall be for you and for them for food.

**22** And Noah did according to all that God commanded him; so he did.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of three manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 6 spans Folios 4r–4v. Folio 4r (page 001 of this chapter) is a three-column manuscript page containing the conclusion of Genesis 5 in its upper portions (visible in the same image used for GEN_005, page 002) and the beginning of Genesis 6 in the lower sections; the Genesis 6 text begins at the folio's lower columns with verse 1 and runs through approximately verse 8. Folio 4v (page 002 of this chapter) continues Genesis 6 from approximately verse 9 through verse 22. The Tiberian vocalization — full qamets, patah, tsere, and shewa pointing — is present and legible throughout both folios. The divine name יהוה appears in verses 3, 5, 6, 7, and 8 with full Masoretic qere notation (the vowels of אֲדֹנָי, "Adonai," are supplied to indicate the liturgical reading convention). The divine name אֱלֹהִים (Elohim) appears from verse 9 onward as the chapter shifts into the Noahic toledot unit. The paragraph (parashah) break markers are visible at the major literary junctions.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. The Vaticanus manuscript image for Genesis 6 was read directly from the folio (image labeled GEN_006_vaticanus_page_001.jpg; catalogue reference Vat.gr.1209/0008). The Greek text is written in the characteristic three-column uncial hand of the Vaticanus. The folio is clearly legible throughout. The Vaticanus Genesis 6 presents several noteworthy divergences from the MT that are documented in full below, including the rendering of בְּנֵי הָאֱלֹהִים (sons of God) as οἱ ἄγγελοι τοῦ θεοῦ (the angels of God) in some LXX manuscript traditions (though Vaticanus itself reads υἱοὶ τοῦ θεοῦ, "sons of God," matching the MT), the crux at verse 3, and the rendering of נֶפִלִים (Nephilim) as γίγαντες (giants).

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. הָאָדָם (ha-adam) — "the human beings"** (throughout)

Genesis 6 opens a new phase in the Genesis narrative, and the noun הָאָדָם (ha-adam, with the definite article) is used throughout the opening section (vv. 1–7) for the collectively understood human race. In verses 1–2, the text reads כִּי רַב הָאָדָם ("that the human beings multiplied") and בְּנוֹת הָאָדָם ("daughters of the human beings"). The use of הָאָדָם here is the same generic collective employed in Genesis 1:26 and echoed in the toledot formula of 5:1–2, and it is deliberately distinguished from the proper name אָדָם (Adam, the individual patriarch of Genesis 5).

The formal translation renders הָאָדָם as "the human beings" (plural in English), which accurately captures the collective sense. "Man" (KJV, ESV) preserves the formal singular but loses the collective gender-inclusive character of the Hebrew at this point; "mankind" (NASB) introduces an archaic collective. "Human beings" is the most formally precise modern rendering. The phrase בְּנוֹת הָאָדָם — literally "daughters of the adam/human-being" — is rendered "daughters of the human beings" throughout, in structural parallel with בְּנֵי הָאֱלֹהִים (sons of God) in the same verses.

**2. בְּנֵי הָאֱלֹהִים (b'nei ha-Elohim) — "sons of God"** (v. 2, 4)

This is one of the most debated phrases in the entire Hebrew Bible. The MT reads בְּנֵי הָאֱלֹהִים — literally "sons of the God" (with the definite article on Elohim). The phrase occurs again in Job 1:6, 2:1, and 38:7, where the context unambiguously refers to members of the divine council — supernatural beings in the heavenly court of God. The three principal interpretive traditions are:

- **The divine-beings reading:** The sons of God are celestial beings — members of the divine council, angels, or "sons of God" in the sense of supernatural figures who belong to God's heavenly retinue. This reading is supported by the Job parallels, by the LXX tradition which renders the phrase in many manuscripts as οἱ ἄγγελοι τοῦ θεοῦ ("the angels of God"), by the Aramaic Targum Neofiti ("sons of the angels"), by 1 Enoch 6–8 (which develops this reading extensively), and by the NT citation in Jude 6–7 and 2 Peter 2:4, which appear to presuppose this tradition.

- **The Sethite reading:** The sons of God are the godly descendants of Seth (from Genesis 5), and the daughters of human beings are the ungodly descendants of Cain (from Genesis 4). This reading is favored by patristic interpreters (Augustine, Chrysostom) and by many modern evangelical interpreters who find the divine-beings reading theologically problematic. It requires taking "sons of God" in the transferred sense of "godly men" — a usage possible in the Hebrew Bible (Ps 82:6; Hos 1:10) but without the specific parallel of בְּנֵי הָאֱלֹהִים in the same divine-council sense.

- **The royal/ruling-class reading:** The sons of God are the ruling aristocracy — kings or princes of the ancient world who take multiple wives from among commoners. This reading connects the passage to ancient Near Eastern royal ideology but is the least well-supported linguistically.

The formal translation renders the phrase as "sons of God," which is the literal Hebrew and is the most formally faithful rendering. It does not adjudicate between the three interpretive options; the phrase itself is unambiguous as to its form and is maximally ambiguous as to its referent — which is precisely the character of the Hebrew text. "Sons of God" is the rendering of KJV, ESV, NASB, and JPS. The NIV renders "sons of God" in a footnote while using "sons of God" in the main text (2011 edition); earlier editions used "sons of God" exclusively.

The LXX (Vaticanus) at this verse reads υἱοὶ τοῦ θεοῦ — "sons of God" — matching the MT precisely. However, several other LXX manuscript traditions (notably Codex Alexandrinus and the Vetus Latina) read οἱ ἄγγελοι τοῦ θεοῦ ("the angels of God"), which reflects an interpretive tradition that understood בְּנֵי הָאֱלֹהִים as a reference to heavenly beings. This variant reading in the LXX tradition was likely the form known to the authors of 1 Enoch and to the NT writers.

The Dead Sea Scrolls are relevant here. 4QDeutj (4Q37) preserves a text of Deuteronomy 32:8 that reads בְּנֵי אֱלֹהִים ("sons of God/gods") where the MT reads בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל ("sons of Israel") — a difference that corroborates the existence of an ancient text tradition in which celestial beings called "sons of God" formed a divine council, each assigned to a nation. The 4QDeutj reading is now broadly accepted as the older text. This background strengthens the divine-beings reading of Genesis 6:2, 4.

**3. יָדוֹן (yadon) — the great crux of verse 3**

The verb יָדוֹן in verse 3 — "My spirit shall not **yadon** with the human beings forever" — is one of the most contested words in the Hebrew Bible. It is a hapax legomenon in this form, and its root and meaning have been disputed since antiquity.

The principal proposals are:

- **Root דין (din, "to judge, to contend, to plead a legal case"):** This yields "My spirit shall not contend/strive with the human beings forever" (KJV "strive"; NASB "strive"; some read "contend"). This reading takes yadon as a Qal of דין with the nun assimilated or as a contracted form. The sense would be that the Spirit of God has been striving with — contending against, pleading with — corrupt humanity, and will do so no longer.

- **Root דון or דנן (related to Arabic dana, "to remain, to dwell"):** This yields "My spirit shall not remain/abide in the human beings forever." Some scholars (Westermann, Speiser) prefer this reading, connecting it to the Spirit of God as life-breath dwelling within mortal flesh: the divine breath that animates human life (as in Gen 2:7) will not remain permanently within creatures of flesh.

- **Root שׁוֹט or שׁדד ("to rule, to shield, to judge"):** Less commonly proposed; the Greek and Syriac traditions suggest the translators saw something like "rule" or "govern."

The LXX (Vaticanus) renders verse 3 as: οὐ μὴ καταμείνῃ τὸ πνεῦμά μου ἐν τοῖς ἀνθρώποις τούτοις εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα — "My spirit shall not remain/abide in these human beings forever." The LXX uses καταμείνῃ (from καταμένω, "to remain, to abide"), which points to the "remain/dwell" reading of the root. The LXX's use of "remain" (καταμένω) rather than "strive" (κρίνω or similar) reflects either a different Hebrew Vorlage or an interpretive decision to read the verb as referring to the Spirit's dwelling within mortal flesh.

The Targum Onkelos renders: "My spirit shall not contend with the sons of men forever, because they are flesh." Targum Pseudo-Jonathan: "My spirit shall not contend with human beings." Both Targums favor the "contend/strive" reading.

The DSS have not yielded a clear parallel text for this verse that resolves the crux.

The formal translation renders: "My spirit shall not **contend** with the human beings forever, because he is flesh." The choice of "contend" is made on the following grounds: (1) it is the most formally close to the widely attested root דין, which elsewhere clearly means "to judge, to plead, to contend at law"; (2) it preserves the legal-contention sense that is thematically apt — the Spirit has been striving against human wickedness, making a divine case against the corruption of Genesis 6:5; (3) it is less interpretively flat than "remain," which makes the verse a comment on human mortality rather than a statement of divine restraint. However, the "remain" reading is fully noted as the reading supported by the LXX (Vaticanus) and should not be dismissed: it reads as: "My spirit shall not remain [dwell as animating breath] in mortal flesh forever" — introducing the 120-year limit as a limit on human lifespan rather than (as in the contend-reading) a period of grace before judgment.

The phrase בְּשַׁגַּם הוּא בָשָׂר ("because he is flesh") presents its own difficulty: בְּשַׁגַּם is grammatically obscure. Some ancient interpreters read it as an Aramaic borrowing or as a combined particle (be + shagam, "in that also," or "inasmuch as"). It is rendered "because he is flesh" following the majority formal equivalence tradition.

The "one hundred and twenty years": this has been interpreted either as (a) a period of grace before the flood — 120 years until judgment — or (b) a new maximum human lifespan (contradicted by subsequent lifespans of Shem and others in Genesis 11, which exceed 120 years). Interpretation (a) is the more defensible reading in context, given the immediately following flood narrative.

**4. נְפִלִים (Nephilim) — "the Nephilim"** (v. 4)

The term נְפִלִים (Nephilim) appears only twice in the Hebrew Bible: here in Genesis 6:4 and in Numbers 13:33, where the Israelite spies report seeing the Nephilim in Canaan (descendants of Anak). In Numbers 13:33 the spies say: "We were in our own eyes as grasshoppers, and so we were in their eyes" — the Nephilim are presented as beings of terrifying stature.

The root is almost certainly נָפַל (naphal, "to fall"). The most commonly proposed derivation is either:
- "Those who fall [upon others]" — warriors, those who fall violently, attackers (reading Nephilim as agentive)
- "Those who have fallen" — fallen ones, beings who have come down from a higher realm (reading Nephilim as passive, and connecting to the divine-beings interpretation of v. 2)
- "Giants" — the LXX rendering γίγαντες ("giants") reflects either a reading of the root or a separate tradition about the physical stature of these beings

The LXX (Vaticanus) renders נְפִלִים as οἱ γίγαντες — "the giants." This rendering was enormously influential in the subsequent Christian tradition. The Vulgate follows suit with gigantes. However, "giants" is an interpretive gloss rather than a formal equivalent; the Hebrew word is a transliterated proper noun or group designation whose meaning is not fully transparent.

The formal translation retains "the Nephilim" as a transliterated proper noun, following ESV, NASB, and JPS. The word is not translated but is brought across from Hebrew into English as a technical term, as is appropriate for a formal equivalence translation of a term whose meaning is uncertain and whose referent is highly specific.

The clause structure of verse 4 is complex: "The Nephilim were on the earth in those days, and also afterward, when the sons of God came in to the daughters of the human beings and they bore children to them." This "and also afterward" (וְגַם אַחֲרֵי כֵן) is significant: it indicates that the Nephilim existed both before and after the specific episode of the sons of God and the daughters of human beings. This may explain the Numbers 13:33 reference to Nephilim after the flood, though the narrative logic remains an exegetical challenge.

The phrase הַגִּבֹּרִים אֲשֶׁר מֵעוֹלָם אַנְשֵׁי הַשֵּׁם — "the mighty men who were of old, the men of renown" — refers to these offspring. The word גִּבֹּרִים (gibborim) denotes warriors, champions, mighty men. אַנְשֵׁי הַשֵּׁם literally means "men of the name" — i.e., men whose names are remembered, men of fame. "Renown" (from Old French renon, "name again") captures this sense: to have renown is to be named repeatedly, to be on the lips of those who retell legends.

**5. יֵצֶר (yetser) — "inclination" / "imagination"** (v. 5)

Verse 5 is the theological diagnosis of the human condition that precipitates the flood: "every inclination (יֵצֶר) of the thoughts of his heart was only evil all the day." The word יֵצֶר (yetser) derives from the root יָצַר (yatsar, "to form, to shape, to fashion") — the same root used in Genesis 2:7 for God's forming (יַיִּצֶר) of the human being from the dust. The yetser is that which is formed or shaped within the human being — the formed tendency, the shaped inclination, the inner impulse.

In rabbinic theology, the יֵצֶר הַרַּע (yetser ha-ra, "the evil inclination") became a major theological concept: the congenital human tendency toward self-interest, desire, and evil that must be disciplined through Torah observance. This verse is the proof-text for the concept. The formal translation renders יֵצֶר as "inclination" (following ESV, JPS) rather than "imagination" (KJV) or "intent" (NASB). "Imagination" (KJV) reflects the Latin imaginatio of the Vulgate but does not capture the formative, shaped character of the Hebrew word. "Inclination" preserves the sense of something tilted or disposed in a particular direction — formed that way.

The phrase כָּל יֵצֶר מַחְשְׁבֹת לִבּוֹ רַק רַע כָּל הַיּוֹם — "every inclination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil all the day" — is one of the most sweeping statements of radical human corruption in the Bible. Every (כָּל) inclination, only (רַק) evil, all (כָּל) the day: the triplicate quantifiers ("every," "only," "all") allow no exception and no intermission. The formal translation preserves this accumulation without softening.

**6. וַיִּנָּחֶם יְהוָה (vayyinnachem YHWH) — "the LORD was grieved"** (vv. 6–7)

The verb נָחַם (nacham, Niphal) in verse 6 — וַיִּנָּחֶם יְהוָה כִּי עָשָׂה אֶת הָאָדָם בָּאָרֶץ — presents one of the most theologically charged and exegetically discussed verbs in the Old Testament. The Niphal of nacham carries a semantic range spanning: "to be grieved, to be sorry, to regret, to be comforted, to relent, to change one's mind." Here the LORD is the subject, and the object of the verbal action is the making of the human beings: "the LORD was grieved/repented/was sorry that He had made the human beings on the earth."

The theological problem is ancient: the verse appears to say that God changed his mind, experienced grief or regret, was moved to an emotional state that altered his course of action. This seems in tension with the divine attribute of immutability (impassibility) as developed in classical theology, and also with verses like Numbers 23:19 and 1 Samuel 15:29, which explicitly state that God does not "repent/change his mind" (lo yinnachem, the same verb in the Niphal negative). How can God repent/be grieved here and yet Numbers says God does not repent?

The classical theological resolution (following Augustine, Aquinas, Calvin) is that the language of divine grief and repentance is anthropopathic — it uses human emotional categories to express a divine reality that transcends human categories, specifically the reality that God's response to human sin is a genuine response, that the turn toward judgment is not arbitrary or capricious but is a real and weighty divine movement, expressed in terms human beings can understand. The anthropopathic interpretation does not deny that God genuinely responded to human sin; it denies that this response requires a change in God's eternal nature or decree.

The formal translation renders וַיִּנָּחֶם as "was grieved" in verse 6 and "am grieved" in verse 7 (כִּי נִחַמְתִּי כִּי עֲשִׂיתִם, "for I am grieved that I have made them"). "Grieved" is chosen over "sorry" (NIV), "repented" (KJV), or "was sorry" (ESV, NRSV) because it preserves the emotional weight of the Hebrew verb while being more formally controlled than "repented" (which imports too much of the repentance-from-sin connotation in English) and more precise than "sorry" (which is too colloquial). The accompanying phrase וַיִּתְעַצֵּב אֶל לִבּוֹ — literally "and He was pained/grieved unto His heart" — uses the Hithpael of עָצַב (atsab, "to pain, to grieve, to be hurt"). The formal translation renders this "He was pained in His heart," preserving the Hebrew body-metaphor of the heart as the seat of divine emotional response.

**7. The nacham / Noah Wordplay** (vv. 6–8)

The juxtaposition of the verb נָחַם (nacham, "to be grieved/to repent") in verses 6–7 with the name נֹחַ (Noah) in verse 8 is almost certainly deliberate. The chapter opens the Noahic section with the LORD's grief (nacham) and immediately pivots to the man whose name evokes both rest (nuach) and comfort/consolation (nacham). This is the same wordplay identified by Lamech at Genesis 5:29, where Lamech names Noah saying "this one will bring us relief (yenachamenu)" — and the same root surfaces here as the word for God's grief at verse 6.

The nacham-wordplay performs a theological function: the very word that describes the LORD's grief (nacham) is acoustically present in the name of the one who will become the instrument of the LORD's covenant mercy and the restoration of the human line after judgment. The grief of God (nacham) and the grace of Noah (chen — see below) together define the pivot of the narrative.

**8. חֵן בְּעֵינֵי יְהוָה (chen b'einei YHWH) — "favor in the eyes of the LORD"** (v. 8)

Verse 8 is one of the most structurally important verses in Genesis: "But Noah found favor (חֵן, chen) in the eyes of the LORD." The word חֵן (chen) means "favor, grace, kindness" — it is the noun related to the verb חָנַן (chanan, "to be gracious, to show favor"). The verse is the pivot between the account of human corruption (vv. 1–7) and the Noahic toledot (v. 9ff): before any account of Noah's righteousness is given, it is stated that Noah found favor — grace — in the LORD's eyes.

The anagram-play between חֵן (chen, "grace/favor") and נֹחַ (Noah) has been noted by Jewish commentators since antiquity: the two letters of חֵן (chet-nun) are the reverse of the two letters of נֹחַ (nun-chet). "Noah found chen" — the name of the man and the word for the grace he found are mirror images of each other in Hebrew. This formal palindromic relationship between Noah and grace is not accidental: it is the narrator's way of marking Noah as the embodiment of the very quality that defines his relationship to God.

The formal translation renders חֵן as "favor" (following ESV, NASB, JPS) rather than "grace" (KJV margin) or "approval" (various). "Favor" preserves the relational context — finding favor in someone's eyes is a social and legal idiom for being regarded with goodwill — while "grace" (though theologically precise in the New Testament sense of charis) risks importing Pauline connotations that go beyond the immediate narrative meaning. The formal translation notes the chen/Noah wordplay here rather than attempting to render it in English, where it cannot be reproduced.

**9. אֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת נֹחַ (elleh toledot Noach) — "these are the generations of Noah"** (v. 9)

Verse 9 contains the third occurrence of the toledot formula in Genesis: אֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת נֹחַ — "These are the generations of Noah." The toledot formula is the structural spine of the book of Genesis, dividing it into ten sections (traditionally identified by scholars in the Documentary Hypothesis tradition and by others as the literary architecture of the final form of the text). The third toledot is notable:

- The first toledot (2:4): "These are the generations of the heavens and the earth"
- The second toledot (5:1): "This is the book of the generations of Adam"
- The third toledot (6:9): "These are the generations of Noah"

The third toledot formula is followed immediately — unusually — by a character description rather than a genealogy: "Noah was a righteous man; he was blameless in his generations. Noah walked with God." The "generations" that follow are not primarily a genealogical list (Noah's three sons were already listed at 5:32) but a narrative account of what Noah's generations produced — the flood narrative and the covenant. The toledot formula here marks the beginning of the major narrative unit of Genesis 6:9–9:29.

The phrase בְּדֹרֹתָיו (b'dorotav, "in his generations") is significant: it is a plural of דּוֹר (dor, "generation, age, period"). "In his generations" — among the people of his time, in the context of the age in which he lived. The formal translation renders this "in his generations" following the literal Hebrew. The phrase has been read as qualifying Noah's righteousness (he was righteous relative to his corrupt generation, but perhaps not absolutely) or as amplifying it (he was blameless through all the generations of his long life). The formal translation leaves the ambiguity intact.

The phrase הִתְהַלֶּךְ נֹחַ אֶת הָאֱלֹהִים — "Noah walked with God" — uses the identical verbal form and preposition as the Enoch formulae of 5:22 and 5:24. The Hithpael of הָלַךְ with אֶת and the name for God: the same phrase is now applied to Noah, connecting him explicitly to Enoch as one who sustained a habitual, covenantal, relational proximity to God while the surrounding world descended into corruption.

**10. חָמָס (chamas) — "violence"** (vv. 11, 13)

The word חָמָס (chamas) — "violence, wrongdoing, injustice" — occurs twice in verses 11–13 and is the summary characterization of what has corrupted the earth: וַתִּמָּלֵא הָאָרֶץ חָמָס ("the earth was filled with violence"). The word חָמָס in the Hebrew Bible encompasses both physical violence and moral wrongdoing — it is used for violent assault (Ps 11:5), judicial injustice (Amos 3:10), and general moral corruption. The formal translation renders it "violence" (following ESV, NASB, NIV, JPS), which captures the forceful, destructive dimension of the word.

The word occurs again at verse 13: כִּי מָלְאָה הָאָרֶץ חָמָס מִפְּנֵיהֶם ("for the earth is filled with violence through them"). The "through them" (מִפְּנֵיהֶם, literally "from before them," "because of them," "on account of them") links the violence explicitly to the corrupt "all flesh" of verse 12.

**11. עֲצֵי גֹפֶר (atsei gopher) — "gopher wood"** (v. 14)

The word גֹּפֶר (gopher) is a hapax legomenon — it appears only once in the entire Hebrew Bible, here in verse 14. Its meaning is unknown. Ancient translators guessed: the LXX (Vaticanus) renders it ξύλων τετραγώνων ("squared/hewn timbers"), which describes a process of timber preparation rather than a species. The Vulgate renders it lignis laevigatis ("smoothed/planed timbers"). Targum Onkelos uses qadros, suggesting cedar or a similar resinous wood. Various modern proposals have included cypress (the most commonly proposed, on the basis of alleged cognates in other Semitic languages), cedar, pine, laminated wood, and reeds. None has been definitively established.

The formal translation retains "gopher wood" as a transliterated hapax — bringing the unknown Hebrew word into English rather than substituting a guess. This is the approach of ESV, KJV, NASB, and JPS. The NIV uses "cypress wood" with a footnote acknowledging the uncertainty. To render a hapax by a specific species would be to misrepresent the state of knowledge: the text uses an otherwise unattested word, and intellectual honesty requires that the translation represent this uncertainty.

The LXX's rendering (ξύλων τετραγώνων, "squared timbers") is discussed above; the Vaticanus text was read directly and confirms this reading in the uncial hand.

**12. צֹהַר (tsohar) — the window/roof-light of verse 16**

The word צֹהַר (tsohar) in verse 16 is a second hapax or near-hapax. The root צהר appears to be related to שַׁהֲרַיִם or to terms for noon/bright light (cf. צָהֳרַיִם, "noon," the bright time of day). The meaning of צֹהַר in verse 16 has been interpreted as:

- A window or opening to admit light (the traditional interpretation reflected in KJV "window," NIV "roof," NRSV "roof")
- A skylight or translucent covering — perhaps of semi-transparent stone (as suggested in rabbinic tradition: a precious stone called the tsohar that glowed in the dark of the ark)
- A roof-overhang or deck covering
- A cubit-wide opening running the length of the ark's roof

The phrase that follows — "and to a cubit you shall finish it from above" — suggests that the tsohar is measured vertically (one cubit high or one cubit from the top) rather than horizontally, pointing toward some kind of opening or light source near the roof. The LXX (Vaticanus) renders it ἐπισυνάγων ποιήσεις τὴν κιβωτόν ("making the ark with a gathering/convergence") — which seems to interpret the word as describing a tapered or converging roof structure rather than a window. This LXX rendering is unique and difficult; the Vaticanus text was read directly.

Given the genuine uncertainty, the formal translation retains the Hebrew word transliterated — "tsohar" — in the English text, with this note explaining the options. This is unusual in formal equivalence practice (most translations make a choice), but "tsohar" is the most honest representation of the text's state. The verse is rendered: "A tsohar you shall make for the ark, and to a cubit you shall finish it from above."

**13. קֵץ כָּל בָּשָׂר (qets kol basar) — "the end of all flesh"** (v. 13)

The phrase קֵץ כָּל בָּשָׂר בָּא לְפָנַי — "the end of all flesh has come before me" — is the divine announcement of the flood's purpose. קֵץ (qets) means "end, terminus" and in later apocalyptic literature becomes a technical term for the eschatological end (as in Daniel). Here it is used in its more concrete sense: the determined end of the corrupt epoch of "all flesh" has arrived in God's sight. The phrase "has come before me" (בָּא לְפָנַי) uses the spatial idiom of things coming into the divine presence as if presented before a judge. The end has entered God's court: the sentence will now be executed. The formal translation renders this literally.

**14. Verse 22 — The formal conclusion**

The final verse of Genesis 6 — וַיַּעַשׂ נֹחַ כְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה אֹתוֹ אֱלֹהִים כֵּן עָשָׂה — "And Noah did according to all that God commanded him; so he did" — is a formal compliance formula. The repetition "so he did" (כֵּן עָשָׂה) after "according to all that God commanded him" (כְּכֹל אֲשֶׁר צִוָּה) is emphatic: Noah did it all, and he did it exactly. The formula anticipates the repeated use of the same formula in Exodus for the tabernacle construction (Exod 39:32, 39:43, 40:16), where "according to all that the LORD commanded Moses, so they did" marks the faithful completion of God's instruction. Noah's obedience at the close of Genesis 6 is thus structurally parallel to Israel's obedience at the close of Sinai: the same pattern of divine command followed by human faithful compliance. The translation renders this literally, preserving the doubled affirmation.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 6

The Vaticanus manuscript image for Genesis 6 was read directly. The following variants and divergences from the MT are noted:

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 6:2 | בְּנֵי הָאֱלֹהִים ("sons of God") | υἱοὶ τοῦ θεοῦ ("sons of God") | "sons of God" | Vaticanus matches MT; other LXX MSS (Alexandrinus, Vetus Latina) read ἄγγελοι ("angels") |
| 6:3 | לֹא יָדוֹן רוּחִי ("My spirit shall not yadon") | οὐ μὴ καταμείνῃ τὸ πνεῦμά μου ("My spirit shall not remain") | "shall not contend" | LXX "remain" (καταμένω) supports the dwell/abide reading; MT supports the contend/strive reading |
| 6:4 | נְפִלִים ("Nephilim") | οἱ γίγαντες ("the giants") | "the Nephilim" | LXX interpretive rendering; "Nephilim" retained as formal transliteration |
| 6:6 | וַיִּנָּחֶם יְהוָה ("the LORD was grieved/repented") | καὶ ἐνεθυμήθη ὁ θεός ("and God considered/thought over it") | "the LORD was grieved" | LXX uses ἐνθυμέομαι ("to ponder, to consider") — a more intellectualist rendering avoiding the anthropopathic emotional grief; MT is retained |
| 6:6 | וַיִּתְעַצֵּב אֶל לִבּוֹ ("He was pained in His heart") | καὶ διενοήθη ("and He considered/reflected") | "He was pained in His heart" | LXX omits the heart-phrase; MT is more corporeal/anthropopathic |
| 6:14 | עֲצֵי גֹפֶר ("gopher wood") | ξύλων τετραγώνων ("squared timbers") | "gopher wood" | LXX interprets the hapax as a description of timber preparation; MT hapax retained |
| 6:16 | צֹהַר ("tsohar") | ἐπισυνάγων ποιήσεις ("making it convergent/gathered") | "tsohar" (transliterated) | LXX interprets as a tapered roof structure; MT hapax retained transliterated |
| 6:19 | שְׁנַיִם מִכֹּל ("two of every kind") | δύο δύο ("two by two") | "two of every kind" | LXX distributive reduplication; MT is singular pair-per-kind instruction |

---

### DSS Note — Genesis 6

The Dead Sea Scrolls include several fragmentary texts relevant to Genesis 6:

- **4QGenb (4Q2)**: Fragmentary, preserves portions of Genesis 6. The text agrees with the MT against no major variants.
- **4QGenesisd (4Q4)**: Fragments overlap with Genesis 6 material. No significant variants from MT.
- **1QapGen (1Q20, the Genesis Apocryphon)**: A Jewish Aramaic expansion of Genesis 1–15, including an extensive retelling of the Noah narrative. The Genesis Apocryphon expands the story of Genesis 6 dramatically, but it is a literary paraphrase/expansion rather than a manuscript of the text itself, and thus does not constitute a textual variant. It does confirm that the divine-beings interpretation of Genesis 6:1–4 was known and elaborated in Second Temple Judaism.
- **4QEnoch (4Q201–202, 4Q204–206)**: Aramaic fragments of 1 Enoch, which represents the most extensive Second Temple elaboration of the Genesis 6:1–4 narrative. The Watchers (a designation for the בְּנֵי הָאֱלֹהִים not found in the Genesis text but elaborated from it) descend, take wives, produce the Nephilim, and are imprisoned. The Enochic tradition treats Genesis 6:1–4 as the central cosmic event behind human corruption and the flood.

### Structural and Theological Note

Genesis 6 is the most complex chapter of the Genesis prologue in terms of literary structure, theological density, and textual difficulty. It performs four distinct narrative functions simultaneously:

**1. Transition from the toledot of Adam to the toledot of Noah (vv. 1–8 / v. 9)**

The chapter opens as the conclusion of the second major toledot unit (the book of the generations of Adam, 5:1–6:8) and pivots at verse 9 into the third toledot unit (the generations of Noah, 6:9–9:29). The literary seam is precisely at the chapter midpoint: verses 1–8 conclude the Adamic era, and verse 9 opens the Noahic era. This structural double function of Genesis 6 — simultaneously closing one toledot and opening another — is unique in Genesis.

**2. The Cosmic Disruption (vv. 1–4)**

The opening four verses describe an event that is cosmologically and narratively unprecedented in Genesis: the crossing of a boundary between the divine and human realms, producing offspring of anomalous character (the Nephilim, the mighty men of renown). Whether the "sons of God" are understood as divine beings, Sethite descendants, or rulers, the narrative function of the passage is to describe the breakdown of created order — the blurring of the boundary between heaven and earth — as the epitome of the corruption that merits the flood. The divine response (v. 3) limits the human lifespan (or announces a period of grace), placing a divine restraint on the disordered world.

**3. The Divine Grief and the Double Nacham (vv. 5–8)**

The LORD's response to human wickedness (vv. 5–7) is expressed in the most anthropopathic language in the Pentateuch: God is grieved (vayinnachem), God is pained in His heart (vayyitatsev el libbo). This language of divine emotional response sets the theological register of the flood narrative: the destruction of the flood is not the act of an impassive cosmic judge but the response of a Creator who has been genuinely grieved by the corruption of what He made. The grief is real; the response is proportionate to the grief.

Against this divine grief, the name of Noah sounds as a counter-note: the man whose name means rest (nuach), who is found with favor/grace (chen — the anagram of his own name), stands as the pivot point between the world that God is grieving and the world that God will preserve.

**4. The Noahic Commission and the Covenant Announcement (vv. 9–22)**

From verse 9, the chapter shifts into a new literary register: the detailed divine instruction for the ark. God speaks to Noah with the precision of an architect — dimensions, materials, design, purpose — and the narrative tone becomes specific, practical, and covenantal. The first mention of "covenant" (בְּרִיתִי, beriti — "my covenant") in the entire Bible appears at verse 18: "I will establish my covenant with you." The covenant is not yet described (it will be fully articulated in Genesis 9:8–17), but its announcement here establishes that the flood narrative is not merely a story of judgment and survival but a covenantal transaction: God is not abandoning the creation but reforming it, and Noah is the covenant partner through whom the human line continues.

The divine name in this chapter shifts with the toledot boundary: verses 1–8 consistently use יהוה (the LORD), the covenantal-relational name associated with Noah's "finding favor in the eyes of the LORD." From verse 9 onward, the name אֱלֹהִים (God) predominates — the Creator God who gives the ark instructions and makes the covenant. This divine-name pattern is one of the key data points in the Documentary Hypothesis analysis of Genesis, where the LORD-sections are assigned to the J source and the God-sections to the P source. The formal translation does not adjudicate source questions but preserves the divine-name pattern of the MT exactly: "the LORD" for יהוה, "God" for אֱלֹהִים, with no harmonization between them.

The chapter closes with the compliance formula of verse 22, which is not merely a statement of Noah's obedience but a theological declaration: against the universal corruption of "all flesh" in verses 5–13, one man did according to all that God commanded him. The structural contrast is absolute: every inclination of every human heart was only evil all the day — and Noah did all that God commanded, exactly so.

— LOGOS
